docs.title: Welcome to My Learning Journey
docs.text: Hi! I'm Phung, and this is where I document my thoughts, learnings, and ongoing projects.

docs.title: What to expect
docs.text:
• Here I'll be sharing:
    • Technical deep dives and tutorials.
    • Notes on new technologies I'm exploring.
    • Project showcases and behind-the-scenes.

docs.title: Join Strategies in Spark
docs.text: Apache Spark provides several join strategies to efficiently combine datasets. The choice of strategy depends on data size, partitioning, and available resources.

docs.title: 1. Broadcast Hash Join
docs.text:
• When used: One dataset is small enough to fit in memory.
  • How it works: The smaller dataset is broadcast to all worker nodes, and each node joins it with its partition of the larger dataset.
  • Pros: Fast, avoids shuffling large data.
  • Cons: Limited by memory size.

docs.title: 2. Shuffle Hash Join
docs.text:
• When used: Both datasets are large, but at least one can fit in memory after shuffling.
  • How it works: Both datasets are shuffled on the join key, and a hash table is built for one side.
  • Pros: Efficient for medium-sized data.
  • Cons: Involves shuffling, which can be expensive.

docs.title: 3. Sort Merge Join
docs.text:
• When used: Both datasets are large and already sorted or can be sorted efficiently.
  • How it works: Datasets are shuffled and sorted on the join key, then merged.
  • Pros: Scales to very large datasets.
  • Cons: Sorting and shuffling can be costly.

docs.title: 4. Cartesian Join
docs.text:
• When used: No join keys are specified.
  • How it works: Every row of one dataset is joined with every row of the other.
  • Pros: Rarely used intentionally.
  • Cons: Extremely expensive; avoid if possible.

docs.title: Choosing a Strategy
docs.text:
• Spark automatically selects the join strategy based on data size and configuration.
  • You can influence the strategy using hints (e.g., broadcast()), or by tuning Spark settings.

docs.title: Optimize tips preface
docs.text: Sometimes you run into issues with spark such as OOM, shuffle spill, etc. This section outlines some tips to optimize your spark job.

docs.title: Partition Tuning in Spark
docs.text: Partition tuning is crucial for optimizing the performance of Spark jobs. It involves adjusting the number and size of partitions to balance workload and resource utilization.

docs.title: Why Partition Tuning Matters
docs.text:
• Parallelism: More partitions allow better parallel processing.
  • Resource Utilization: Proper partitioning prevents resource underutilization or excessive overhead.
  • Shuffle Efficiency: Reduces data movement and network I/O during shuffles.

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Default Partitioning: Spark uses a default number of partitions (e.g., 200 for shuffles).
  • Repartitioning: Use repartition() to increase partitions (causes a full shuffle).
  • Coalescing: Use coalesce() to decrease partitions (minimizes shuffling).

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Input Size: Aim for partition sizes of 100–200 MB for optimal performance.
  • Cluster Resources: Match partitions to the number of available cores.
  • Avoid Small Files: Too many small partitions/files can degrade performance.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Increase partitions
df = df.repartition(100)

# Decrease partitions
df = df.coalesce(10)

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text: Use the Spark UI to monitor stages and tasks, and adjust partitioning as needed.

docs.title: What is RAG?
docs.text: RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. It is a technique that combines the power of retrieval and generation to improve the performance of large language models.

docs.title: Why RAG?
docs.text: • LLMs are trained on a massive dataset, but they don't have access to real-time information.
